pages.label: Welcome
pages.section: What a care plan is and why it matters

items.html:
Welcome to Care Planning

Welcome to this awareness course on care planning. A care plan is the written record of how a person wishes to be supported — what matters to them, what they can do for themselves, where they need help, and the outcomes they hope to achieve. As a care worker in England, you contribute to, follow and help review care plans every day. This course is grounded in the Care Act 2014, the Mental Capacity Act 2005, person-centred planning principles and the expectations of the Care Quality Commission (CQC).

  • Person-centred: the plan begins with the person, not with a list of tasks.
  • Your role: to follow the plan, add your observations, and help keep it current.
  • A living document: a care plan is never finished — it changes as the person does.

What this course covers

Over the next pages you will move from what a care plan is, through what makes a good one, to how you follow, contribute to and help review it in your daily work.

  • The basics: person-centred care, and the difference between assessment, plan and review.
  • A good plan: needs, wishes, outcomes and strengths, capacity and consent, and positive risk-taking.
  • In practice: following the plan, recording changes, reviewing, and escalating when needs change.

This e-learning covers the knowledge element only. It builds your awareness and good practice, but it does not replace the supervised training and formal assessment carried out by an assessor or senior colleague in your workplace. Formal assessment and the authorship of care plans sit with the assessor; your part is to follow them, contribute to them and help review them well.

pages.label: The basics
pages.section: What a care plan is and why it matters

items.html:
Assessment, plan and review

Care planning is not a single document but an ongoing cycle. Understanding the three stages helps you see where your work fits and who is responsible for what. Each stage feeds the next, and the person stays at the centre throughout.

  • Assessment: a trained assessor works out the person's needs and eligibility, as set out in the Care Act 2014.
  • Care and support plan: a written record of how those needs will be met and the outcomes the person wants.
  • Review: a regular check that the plan is still right and still working.

You may not carry out the formal assessment or write the plan, but your daily observations feed directly into both.

Why the difference matters

Knowing which stage you are in keeps everyone clear about their role. The Care Act 2014 places a duty on local authorities to assess a person's needs and, where they are eligible, to produce a care and support plan setting out how those needs will be met. A senior or assessor leads that work, drawing on the person's own views.

Your part comes in following the plan faithfully and feeding back what you see. If support the plan describes no longer suits the person — a task they now manage alone, or a need that has grown — that observation belongs in a review. Understanding the difference between assessing, planning and reviewing tells you when to simply follow the plan and when to speak up so it can be changed properly.

items.taskDescription: Drag each word into the correct gap to complete the sentences about the care planning cycle.
items.textField: Assessment identifies a person's needs, the care *plan* records how those needs will be met, and a *review* checks the plan is still working. Together they form an ongoing *cycle* that keeps the plan a *living* document.

pages.label: Person-centred care
pages.section: What a care plan is and why it matters

items.html:
Putting the person first

Person-centred care means planning support around the individual — their life, their preferences and their goals — rather than fitting the person into a fixed routine. It treats the person as the expert on their own life and as an active partner, not a passive recipient of care.

  • The whole person: their history, relationships, culture, faith and interests all matter.
  • Their voice: what the person wants leads the plan wherever possible.
  • Their strengths: what someone can still do is valued as much as what they cannot.

A care plan is where person-centred care is written down, so that everyone supporting the person works in the same, personalised way.

Why it matters day to day

The Care Act 2014 is built on the idea of wellbeing — a person's dignity, control over daily life, relationships and participation in the community. A person-centred plan protects that wellbeing by keeping support tied to what the individual actually wants, not to what is quickest or most convenient.

For you, working in a person-centred way means reading the plan closely and delivering care the way this particular person prefers: the name they like to be called, the order they get ready in the morning, the cup of tea made just so. These details are not trivial. They are what make care feel respectful rather than routine, and they are exactly the things a good care plan captures. When you honour them, you help the person feel known, valued and in control of their own life.

items.question: What does it mean to deliver care in a person-centred way?

answers.text: Planning and giving support around the individual's own preferences, strengths and goals
answers.feedback: Correct. Person-centred care starts from the person and treats them as a partner in their own support.

answers.text: Following the same fixed routine with everyone so care is consistent
answers.feedback: Not quite. A fixed routine for everyone ignores what makes each person an individual.

answers.text: Deciding what is best for the person because you are the trained worker
answers.feedback: No. Person-centred care values the person's own voice; it is not about deciding for them.

answers.text: Doing tasks as quickly as possible to fit more people into the day
answers.feedback: No. Speed and convenience must never replace support shaped around the individual.

pages.label: Needs and strengths
pages.section: What goes into a good care plan

items.html:
Needs, wishes, outcomes and strengths

A good care plan captures far more than a person's problems. Under the Care Act 2014, planning is outcome-focused — it starts from what the person wants to achieve and how they want to live, then works out the support needed to get there.

  • Needs: the areas of daily life where the person needs help, such as washing, eating or moving safely.
  • Wishes and outcomes: what matters to the person and the goals they want to reach.
  • Strengths: the abilities, resources and relationships the person already has.

Planning around strengths, not just needs, is often called a strengths-based approach, and the Care Act encourages it.

Outcomes, not just tasks

There is an important difference between an outcome and a task. A task is something a worker does — “assist with a shower”. An outcome is what the person gains — “to feel clean, confident and able to see friends”. Good plans lead with outcomes and then describe the support that helps the person reach them.

Building on strengths keeps a person as independent as possible. If someone can butter their own toast but needs help to make the tea, the plan should say so, so that no one takes over the part they can manage. This protects skills, confidence and dignity. As you deliver care you see a person's real strengths and outcomes at work, and when what you see differs from the plan, that is valuable information to feed back.

items.question: Which of these is written as an outcome rather than a task?

answers.text: To be able to get to the community lunch club and stay in touch with friends
answers.feedback: Correct. This describes what the person wants to achieve in their life, which is an outcome.

answers.text: Assist the person to wash and dress each morning
answers.feedback: Not quite. This describes a task a worker does, rather than what the person gains from it.

answers.text: Prompt the person to take their medication at 8am
answers.feedback: No. This is a task; an outcome would describe what the person achieves by staying well.

answers.text: Empty and clean the commode after each use
answers.feedback: No. This is a care task, not an outcome the person is working towards.

pages.label: Involving people
pages.section: What goes into a good care plan

items.html:
Involving the person and their family

A care plan belongs to the person it is about. The Care Act 2014 requires the person to be involved in preparing their own plan, and to have a genuine say in how their needs are met. A plan written without the person is unlikely to reflect what truly matters to them.

  • The person first: their views, wishes and feelings lead the plan.
  • Family and friends: those close to the person can add valuable knowledge, with the person's agreement.
  • Advocacy: where someone would struggle to be involved, an independent advocate can help their voice be heard.

Involvement is not a one-off signature; it continues every time the plan is delivered and reviewed.

Family knowledge and the person's voice

Family members and friends often hold years of knowledge about a person — their routines, their likes and dislikes, what settles them when they are upset. With the person's consent, this information makes a plan richer and more accurate. It is especially valuable when someone finds it hard to express their own wishes.

Even so, the person's own voice always comes first. Family views should support, not override, what the individual wants, and you should never assume a relative speaks for the person unless they have a legal right to do so. In your daily work you help keep people involved: you check preferences rather than assuming them, and you pass on anything new so the plan reflects the person as they are now.

items.question: A person should be actively involved in preparing and reviewing their own care and support plan.
items.correctMsg: Correct. The Care Act 2014 requires the person to be involved, so the plan reflects what matters to them.
items.wrongMsg: Not quite. Under the Care Act 2014 the person must be involved in their own plan; it is not written for them alone.

pages.label: Capacity and consent
pages.section: What goes into a good care plan

items.html:
Capacity, consent and the Mental Capacity Act

Care and support depend on a person's agreement. The Mental Capacity Act 2005 sets out how to support people to make their own decisions and what to do when someone may not be able to. It applies to everyone aged 16 and over in England and Wales.

  • Assume capacity: start from the belief that a person can make their own decisions.
  • Support the decision: give information in a way the person can understand before deciding they cannot choose.
  • Unwise is allowed: a person has the right to make what others see as an unwise choice.

Capacity is decision-specific — a person may manage some decisions and need help with others.

When someone cannot decide

Where a person genuinely cannot make a particular decision, even with support, the Mental Capacity Act requires that any decision made on their behalf is in their best interests and is the least restrictive option available. Formal capacity assessments and best-interests decisions are led by senior staff or professionals, not by you alone.

Your role is still important. You gain consent for the everyday care you give — explaining what you would like to do and checking the person agrees — and you watch for signs that someone's ability to decide may be changing. Consent can be withdrawn at any moment, and you respect that. If you are unsure whether a person understands a choice, or you notice a change in their capacity, report it so it can be looked at properly. Never simply assume that someone cannot decide.

items.question: A person makes a choice about their care that you think is unwise but they clearly understand it. What does the Mental Capacity Act expect?

answers.text: Respect their decision, as a person with capacity has the right to make an unwise choice
answers.feedback: Correct. The Act protects a person's right to make their own decisions, including ones others see as unwise.

answers.text: Overrule them, because your job is to keep them safe
answers.feedback: No. Making an unwise choice does not, on its own, mean a person lacks capacity to decide.

answers.text: Assume they lack capacity because the choice seems unwise
answers.feedback: Not quite. An unwise decision is not proof of lacking capacity; you must still assume capacity.

answers.text: Ask a relative to make the decision for them instead
answers.feedback: No. A relative cannot simply take over a decision a person with capacity is able to make.

pages.label: SMART and risk
pages.section: What goes into a good care plan

items.html:
SMART, personalised outcomes

Outcomes work best when they are clear enough for everyone to know what success looks like. Many services use the idea of SMART outcomes — specific, measurable, achievable, relevant and time-bound — so that goals are realistic and progress can be seen.

  • Specific and personal: tied to this individual, not a general phrase copied between plans.
  • Measurable and achievable: written so you can tell whether the outcome is being reached.
  • Relevant and time-bound: meaningful to the person, with a sensible timescale.

A personalised outcome describes what the person wants to achieve, not simply the tasks a worker carries out.

Risk and positive risk-taking

Good plans deal honestly with risk. Some support carries hazards — a hot bath, a walk outdoors, cooking a meal — and the plan should record how to keep the person as safe as reasonably possible. But safety is not the only goal. Positive risk-taking means weighing a risk against the benefit to the person's independence, choice and quality of life, rather than removing every risk by removing the activity.

Wrapping someone in cotton wool can strip away the very things that give life meaning. A person-centred plan supports someone to keep doing what matters, with sensible measures in place, and records the discussion so the balance is clear. Your job is to follow those agreed measures, and to report if a risk changes — if a walk becomes unsteady, say — so the plan can be updated rather than the freedom taken away.

items.taskDescription: Drag each word into the correct gap to complete the guidance on outcomes and risk.
items.textField: A good outcome is *SMART* and describes what the person wants to *achieve*, not just the tasks a worker performs. Supporting *positive* risk-taking helps a person keep the *independence* and choice that give life meaning.

pages.label: Working to the plan
pages.section: Following and contributing to the plan

items.html:
Following the plan

Once a care plan is agreed, your central duty is to work to it. The plan is there so that everyone supports the person in the same, agreed way, and so that care is safe, consistent and truly personalised. Reading and following it is not optional — it is the foundation of safe practice.

  • Read before you act: know what the plan says for each person you support.
  • Deliver as agreed: follow the person's preferences, routines and any risk measures.
  • Do not freelance: never change the agreed support on your own initiative.

If the plan seems wrong or out of date, you raise it — you do not quietly do something different.

Person-centred delivery

Following the plan and being person-centred go hand in hand. The plan tells you how this individual likes their support given, and delivering it that way — the right pace, the right words, the small preferences noted down — is what turns writing on a page into good care. The CQC expects care to be personalised in exactly this way.

At the same time, a person is more than their plan. Offer everyday choices as you go: which jumper, tea or coffee, now or in ten minutes. Uphold the person's dignity in every task. If a person tells you they now want something done differently, honour reasonable choices where it is safe to do so, and make sure the change is reported so the plan can catch up. Working to the plan never means ignoring the person in front of you.

items.question: You arrive to find a task in the care plan no longer seems to suit the person. What should you do?

answers.text: Follow the agreed plan for now and report your concern so the plan can be reviewed and updated properly
answers.feedback: Correct. You keep to the agreed plan and raise your concern so any change is made properly, not informally.

answers.text: Quietly do the task a different way and say nothing
answers.feedback: No. Changing agreed support on your own, without reporting it, is unsafe and leaves the plan wrong.

answers.text: Skip the task altogether because you disagree with it
answers.feedback: Not quite. You should not simply drop agreed support; raise your concern so it can be looked at.

answers.text: Wait until the annual review months away before mentioning it
answers.feedback: No. Concerns should be reported promptly so the plan can be reviewed when needed, not left for months.

pages.label: Noticing changes
pages.section: Following and contributing to the plan

items.html:
Noticing and recording changes

Because you support a person regularly and closely, you are often the first to notice when something changes. This makes your observations a vital part of care planning. A change may be in the person's health, their abilities, their mood or their circumstances.

  • Health: new pain, poor appetite, changes to skin, or signs of illness.
  • Ability: a task the person now struggles with, or one they have regained.
  • Mood and situation: low spirits, confusion, or a change at home such as a carer becoming unwell.

You do not need to explain the change or diagnose it — your job is to notice it, record it and pass it on.

Your observations feed the plan

A care plan can only stay accurate if the people delivering it feed back what they see. When you record and report a change, you give the team the information they need to review the plan and adjust the support. In this way the plan keeps pace with the person, rather than describing how they used to be.

Because you come to know what is normal for each person, you are well placed to spot what is not. Trust that knowledge: if something feels different, it is worth recording and reporting. Write down what you actually observed — “ate only half of lunch and said they felt sick” — rather than a vague impression. Small, factual observations, gathered over time, are what allow a plan to be reviewed sensibly and kept genuinely useful.

items.question: Over several visits you notice a person needs much more help than usual to walk to the bathroom. What is the best action?

answers.text: Record what you have observed factually and report it so the plan and any risks can be reviewed
answers.feedback: Correct. Factual recording and prompt reporting let the team review the plan and keep the person safe.

answers.text: Say nothing unless the person falls
answers.feedback: No. Waiting for a fall means missing the chance to prevent harm by acting on an early change.

answers.text: Decide it is just old age and not worth mentioning
answers.feedback: Not quite. It is not for you to dismiss a change; record and report it so it can be assessed.

answers.text: Change the person's mobility support yourself without telling anyone
answers.feedback: No. You should not alter agreed support alone; report the change so the plan is updated properly.

pages.label: Working as a team
pages.section: Following and contributing to the plan

items.html:
Communicating with the team

Care planning is a team effort. A person may be supported by several workers, a manager, family, nurses and other professionals, and the care plan is what keeps everyone working together. Clear communication between you all keeps care safe, joined-up and consistent.

  • Handovers: pass on important information clearly at the start and end of your involvement.
  • Records: write in the person's records so the next worker knows what happened.
  • Ask when unsure: check with a senior rather than guessing.

Good teamwork means no important observation is lost between one worker and the next.

Dignity and choice throughout

Whatever the practical demands of the day, dignity and choice run through every part of following a care plan. Dignity means treating the person as a valued individual — using their preferred name, protecting their privacy, and never talking over them or about them as though they were not there. Choice means giving the person a genuine say in their own care, moment to moment.

These are not extras to fit in when time allows; they are central to what the CQC calls caring, responsive support. When you communicate with your team, do so in a way that respects the person's confidentiality, sharing only what colleagues need to know. Delivering the plan with dignity, offering real choices and keeping the team well informed together make care feel respectful and keep the person at its heart.

When and why plans are reviewed

A care plan is a living document, so it must be reviewed to stay useful. A review is a planned check that the support still meets the person's needs and still helps them reach their outcomes. The Care Act 2014 expects plans to be kept under review.

  • Planned reviews: a regular check, often at least once a year, that the plan is still right.
  • Triggered reviews: a review brought forward because something has changed.
  • The person involved: the individual takes part in reviewing their own plan.

A review can confirm the plan is working, adjust it, or set new outcomes as the person's life changes.

Your part in reviews

Reviews are usually led by a senior worker or assessor, but they depend on information from the people who deliver the care. The observations you record between reviews — changes you noticed, outcomes reached, support that no longer fits — are exactly what a review draws on. Without them, a review has little to go on.

A review may be needed sooner than planned when a person's needs change noticeably: after a spell in hospital, a fall, a bereavement, or a steady decline or improvement. You may be asked to share what you have seen, and you should do so honestly and factually. Reviewing keeps a plan accurate and personalised over time, and your steady, everyday recording is what makes an honest review possible.

items.question: A care plan should be reviewed only once, when it is first written.
items.correctMsg: Correct. A care plan is a living document that must be reviewed regularly and whenever needs change.
items.wrongMsg: Not quite. A plan is a living document; it is reviewed regularly and again whenever the person's needs change.

items.html:
Keeping records accurate and timely

Good care planning depends on good records. What you write becomes part of the person's care record, is relied on by the whole team, and can be read by the person, their family, inspectors or a court. The CQC expects records to be accurate, complete and up to date.

  • Accurate and factual: record what you actually saw and did, not opinion or assumption.
  • Timely: write records as soon as you can, while events are fresh.
  • Clear and legible: write plainly so anyone reading can understand exactly what happened.

Sign and date what you write, and never record care you did not give.

Confidentiality and data protection

Care records contain sensitive personal information, so they must be kept confidential and handled in line with data protection law — the UK GDPR and the Data Protection Act 2018. Information about a person should be shared only with those who need it to give safe care, and only with a proper reason.

In practice this means storing records securely, not leaving them where others can see them, and not discussing a person's private details where you might be overheard or with people who have no need to know. If you use a phone or tablet to record care, follow your employer's rules for keeping it secure. Confidentiality is part of a person's dignity: handling their information with care shows the same respect you show in every other part of their support. When you are unsure whether you may share something, ask your manager first.

items.question: Which of these best describes a good care record?

answers.text: Accurate, factual and timely, recording what you saw and did, and kept confidential and secure
answers.feedback: Correct. Good records are factual, prompt and secure, so the team can rely on them and dignity is protected.

answers.text: Written from memory at the end of the week to save time
answers.feedback: No. Records written long after the event are less accurate; they should be completed promptly.

answers.text: Full of your personal opinions about the person and their family
answers.feedback: Not quite. Records should be factual, not opinion, and must respect the person's dignity.

answers.text: Left open on the kitchen table so the next worker can find them
answers.feedback: No. Records hold sensitive information and must be stored securely, not left where others can read them.

items.html:
Escalating when needs change

Sometimes what you notice cannot wait for the next planned review. When a person's needs change in a way that affects their safety or wellbeing, you need to escalate — to raise it promptly with the right person so that action can be taken.

  • Know who to tell: your manager, senior or the on-call service, following your employer's procedure.
  • Act promptly: do not wait if the change could affect the person's safety.
  • Record it too: write down what you saw and what you reported.

Escalating is not going over anyone's head — it is how the team keeps a person safe when things change.

Bringing it together

Well done for completing this awareness course on care planning. You have explored what a care plan is and why it matters, including person-centred care and the cycle of assessment, plan and review; what makes a good plan, from needs, strengths and outcomes to capacity, consent and positive risk-taking; how you follow and contribute to the plan through person-centred delivery and careful recording; and how plans are reviewed, recorded and escalated when needs change. Throughout, the care plan is a living document, and your daily observations help keep it accurate.

Remember the key point from the start: this e-learning covers the knowledge element only. It builds your awareness and good practice, but it does not replace the supervised training and formal assessment carried out by an assessor or senior colleague in your workplace. Formal assessment and the authorship of care plans sit with the assessor; your part is to follow them, contribute to them and help review them well. Speak to your manager about the workplace training and assessment that go alongside this learning.
